title: ‘I’m not trained, I’m not qualified’: a systematic review of the mental health literacy amongst British university academics
abstract: For most university students, their tutors or lecturers play an important role when they are experiencing mental health problems (MHPs), and these academics have a responsibility to support students during this. This review aimed to examine the current mental health literacy (MHL) amongst university academics. A systematic review of studies that explored the MHL of academics at UK universities was conducted. It followed the PRISMA guideline and is registered on PROSPERO. Searches were undertaken on six databases, accessed through EBSCO. The studies were limited to peer-reviewed, academic journals, published from 2014 onwards and in English. A further search of reference lists and related citations was conducted. The studies were assessed using the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme Checklist and the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool. A thematic analysis was conducted following Braun and Clarke’s six steps. Six studies, including qualitative and mixed methods were included. Four main themes were identified: the academics’ ability to recognise MHPs, the academics’ ability to manage MHPs, support that the university provides to academics and adverse effects experienced by the academics from supporting students. This review has found that there is a lack of support from universities for their academics in managing students with MHPs through inadequate training and guidance, due to national policies. Therefore, academics struggle to recognise and manage MHPs in students while experiencing difficulties in managing their own workload and wellbeing. This review recommends that universities should increase their support for academics through training, guidance and accessible support systems.

‘I’m not trained, I’m not qualified’: a systematic review of the 
mental health literacy amongst British university academics
Alizeh Moin
a and Oladapo Akinlotan

aFaculty of Health, Medicine and Social Care, Anglia Ruskin University, Bishop Hall Lane, Chelmsford, UK;

ABSTRACT
For most university students, their tutors or lecturers play an impor­
tant role when they are experiencing mental health problems 
(MHPs), and these academics have a responsibility to support stu­
dents during this. This review aimed to examine the current mental 
health literacy (MHL) amongst university academics. A systematic 
review of studies that explored the MHL of academics at UK uni­
versities was conducted. It followed the PRISMA guideline and is 
registered on PROSPERO. Searches were undertaken on six data­
bases, accessed through EBSCO. The studies were limited to peer- 
reviewed, academic journals, published from 2014 onwards and in 
English. A further search of reference lists and related citations was 
conducted. The studies were assessed using the Critical Appraisal 
Skills Programme Checklist and the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool. 
A thematic analysis was conducted following Braun and Clarke’s six 
steps. Six studies, including qualitative and mixed methods were 
included. Four main themes were identified: the academics’ ability 
to recognise MHPs, the academics’ ability to manage MHPs, support 
that the university provides to academics and adverse effects 
experienced by the academics from supporting students. This 
review has found that there is a lack of support from universities 
for their academics in managing students with MHPs through 
inadequate training and guidance, due to national policies. 
Therefore, academics struggle to recognise and manage MHPs in 
students while experiencing difficulties in managing their own 
workload and wellbeing. This review recommends that universities 
should increase their support for academics through training, gui­
dance and accessible support systems.

1. Introduction
The term ‘mental health literacy’ was first introduced in 1997 by Jorm and colleagues and 
is defined as the ‘knowledge and beliefs about mental health disorders which aid their 
recognition, management or prevention’ (Jorm et al. 1997). It is further referred to as ‘the 
ability to recognise specific disorders; knowing how to seek mental health information; 
knowledge of risk factors and causes, of self-treatments, and of professional help

available; and attitudes that promote recognition and appropriate help-seeking’ (Jorm 
et al. 1997). Mental health literacy (MHL) is vital amongst university academics; as 
G. Hughes et al. (2018) states, academics are often the first point of contact when students 
are facing difficulties because they have a pre-existing relationship, are accessible and 
approachable. In this sense, MHL can also be understood to be the support that aca­
demics provide to their students, whether academics can recognise, are aware of or have 
had training on mental health problems (MHPs).
Universities can be a place where students experience pressure on their mental health 
(Akinlotan and Wagstaff 2025). More academics are encountering students with MHPs, as 
Spear et al. (2020), revealed 96% of academics encountered students with MHPs. In 
a study conducted in 2022 by the mental health charity, Student Minds, 57% of respon­
dents who were students had self-reported a mental health issue; of this percentage, 24% 
have reported having been diagnosed with a mental health condition (Student Minds  
2022). Another study conducted by the National Union of Students on behalf of the All 
Party Parliamentary Group on Students (2015) revealed that 33% of them had experi­
enced suicidal thoughts in the past academic year. This is worrying as mental health 
conditions are worsening in the UK. In only a few years, between 2016/17 to 2022/23, the 
number of undergraduate students at universities across the UK who said they have 
experienced MHPs rose from 6% to 16% (Sanders 2023). There are many reasons for the 
increase in mental health conditions including being away from home, academic pres­
sure, financial pressure, social pressure and the pressure of making friends (Guzder 2024). 
McManus and Gunnell (2020), however, provided a contrasting view in that it is unclear 
whether students at university are experiencing more MHPs than their non-student peers. 
Despite this, academics play a critical role in supporting students with MHPs, and the 
growing mental health needs present in the student population in the UK indicate that 
conducting a systematic review of MHL among academics is both timely and essential.
While there is uncertainty about whether universities have a ‘duty of care’ towards their 
students according to the Parliamentary Research Briefing (2021), there has been pressure 
to place a legal responsibility upon universities regarding this (Universities UK 2023). This 
can be interpreted as ‘not to cause harm by careless acts or omissions’ and as ‘further legal 
duties relating to contract, health and safety, human rights, the safeguarding of vulner­
able adults and equalities legislation’ (Universities UK 2023). Universities often practice 
this by assigning an academic supervisor or tutor to support students. Many of these 
academics have reported seeing an increase in the number of students experiencing 
mental health difficulties, as compared to the past (G. Hughes et al. 2018). Academics have 
stressed their importance as a ‘first point of contact’ for students (Bennett et al. 2024). This 
highlights the need for academics to uphold a level of MHL in order to recognise and 
appropriately manage MHPs in their students.
Bronfenbrenner’s Ecological systems Theory (Bronfenbrenner 1979) can help to under­
stand the various factors that can impact an academic’s MHL. According to McLaren and 
Hawe (2024), an ecological perspective can demonstrate the individual and contextual 
systems and how the two are interdependent. The theory is particularly useful to under­
stand mental health as MHPs involve processes that act at different levels; it allows us to 
view mental health from biological, psychological and sociocultural perspectives (Kendler  
2008). By implementing this theory, the various factors affecting MHL can be made 
evident together with how they are interconnected. At the microsystem, the academic’s 
2
A. MOIN AND O. AKINLOTAN
own personal attitudes and experiences will be affecting their approach. Next, the 
mesosystem shows how processes at the microsystem interact with each other. For 
example, academics’ personal views can impact their relationships and interactions with 
each other, which in turn will affect MHL outcomes -– this can manifest in the workplace 
culture and the support they provide to each other. The ecosystem involves factors such 
as training offered to academics, their workload, policies and guidance offered by the 
university and whether there is funding available for mental health support (McLinden  
2016). Next, at the macrosystem, societal attitudes towards mental health problems, 
national campaigns and policies, cultural ideas and stigma can impact MHL. Finally, at 
the chronosystem, the changes over time affecting MHL can be revealed, the most 
pertinent example being COVID-19 and how that has impacted not just MHPs in students 
but how MHL can be supported by academics in a more digitalised and remote learning 
environment (Idris et al. 2021).
So far, there has been no other systematic review conducted which explores the MHL 
of academics at British universities. Although there have been other similar systematic 
reviews that have been conducted including that by Kristin, Gupta, and Keating (2018) 
and Suwanwong et al. (2024), none of these systematic reviews are specific to academics 
at UK universities supporting MHPs in students. This gap draws attention to the need to 
conduct a systematic review in order to investigate this further in a UK-specific context. 
The choice of utilising a systematic review for this research is based on the understanding 
that it will allow the synthesis of available data in a structured and transparent way to 
answer the research question and inform evidence-based recommendations.
1.1. Objective
The objective of this review is to undertake a systematic review of primary studies to 
explore MHL amongst academics at British universities. The research question for this 
systematic review is: ‘what is the MHL amongst academics at British universities?’.
2. Methodology
2.1. Protocol and registration
This review registered under the PROSPERO protocol on the 11 June 2024, 
(CRD42024541653) and follows guidelines set out by the PRISMA statement (Moher 
et al. 2009).
2.2. Search strategy
An initial search was conducted on 19 June 2024, through the following sources: APA 
PsycInfo, British Education Index, CINAHL Plus with Full Text, ERIC, MEDLINE, Psychology 
and Behavioural Sciences Collection. These were all accessed through EBSCO. A wide 
range of search terms were utilised to access as many articles as possible that conducted 
primary research into the MHL of British academics. The search terms included:

− Mental health literacy, understanding, training, support, recognition, counselling, 
knowledge, insight, awareness, education, competence.
− Academic(s), lecturer(s), professor(s), university teacher(s), faculty(ies), educator(s).
− UK, United Kingdom, Britain, Great Britain, British Isles, England, Scotland, Wales, 
Northern Ireland.
− University(ies), postgraduate(s), graduate(s), higher education, college, master’s, 
doctoral, higher learning institution(s), institution(s), academy(ies).
Appendix A demonstrates the search strategy. These search terms were based on syno­
nyms of MHL and the understanding of mental health issues. The filters that were added 
included limiting the search to being from 2014 and onwards and in the English language. 
Limiting the studies to the last 10 years allows for the recommendations to be relevant 
and specific to the current context. Furthermore, this review is based in the UK, this is due 
to the increasing awareness of MHPs at British universities and so that the findings and 
policy recommendations can be sensitive and specific to the UK’s sociocultural 
environment.
2.3. Inclusion/exclusion criteria
The studies that could be included in this systematic review include those that 
explore British academics’ MHL. The term mental health literacy was operationalised 
to mean the ‘understanding’ or ‘support’ provided for MHPs. For example, during the 
search ‘understanding’ could mean how a MHP is recognised or perceived or the 
awareness surrounding it, while ‘support’ could mean the interventions put forward 
to address that MHP. The term ‘British academic’ was operationalised in the search 
to mean any individual that held a teaching role of any subject within a British 
university and came into contact with university students. This was done by reading 
the text and abstract, and occasionally the full text. The inclusion criteria include 
studies that utilise primary research through either quantitative, qualitative or mixed- 
methods. The studies were peer-reviewed, journal articles, from the UK (England, 
Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland) written in English and published from 2014 
onwards. Please refer to Table 1.
2.4. Selection process and data collection
During the initial search 9816 studies were identified after duplicates were removed. The 
studies were then screened by their title and abstract. Studies were then excluded at this 
stage if they didn’t relate to an academic setting or didn’t explore mental health problems 
or didn’t relate to academics or anyone with a teaching role at a university as apparent 
from the title and abstract. The studies were then reduced from 9816 to 182. After this, full 
text screening was conducted. Studies were excluded at this stage if they were not 
exploring UK universities, or were considering UK universities along with foreign univer­
sities without presenting them separately. Some studies were also excluded if they 
investigated teachers or academics outside of a university (for example a school) or 
explored academics in universities and schools without presenting the information 
4
A. MOIN AND O. AKINLOTAN
separately. They were further excluded if studies were looking at ‘disabilities’ as a whole 
and not separately exploring mental health problems.
To ensure rigour, increase quality and minimise bias, all the steps involved in the search 
strategy including defining the search terms and eligibility criteria, screening of results 
and selection of the final studies were independently completed by the two reviewers 
(authors). After the initial search by the first reviewer, the second reviewer completed an 
independent search using the same search terms on the same databases. Next, the first 
reviewer removed the duplicates and performed an initial screening using the title and 
abstract. This was checked by the second reviewer. A full text screening was then 
completed by the first reviewer, this was independently checked by the second reviewer. 
Finally, the first reviewer came to a conclusion of the selected studies, and these were 
independently checked and agreed to by the second reviewer. Both reviewers performed 
forward and backward searches of these studies by going through any sources that had 
cited the studies and their reference lists. No further studies were identified after this 
process. Any disagreements between the two reviewers were discussed and the two 
reviewers agreed on the final decision.
2.5. Data analysis and synthesis
Once the selected studies were finalised, key study characteristics were extracted and 
inserted onto a table in a Word document. Full-text screening was conducted, and the 
study characteristic were extracted – please refer to Table 2. Critical appraisal of the 
studies was conducted using the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme Checklist (Critical 
Appraisal Skills Programme 2023) – please see Table 3. The appraisal was conducted by 
giving each study a score according to the questions presented in the checklist, with 
a score of 1 for an answer of yes, 0.5 for can’t tell and 0 for no. For the last question 1 was 
given if the study was very valuable, 0.5 if it was moderately valuable and 0 if it wasn’t 
valuable. An average was determined for these scores and multiplied by 100 for 
a percentage; 0–20% would be classed as very poor, 21–40% as poor, 41–60% as 
moderate, 61–80% as good and 81–100% as excellent. These scores were split according 
to proportional grouping to reflect a balanced division across the scoring scale from 
0–100%. The questions the studies were assessed against were related to the studies’ 
aims, whether the methodology was appropriate, the research design, the recruitment 
strategy, the data collection, the relationship between the researcher and the participant, 
ethical issues being considered, rigorous analysis, a clear statement of findings being 
available and how valuable the research is. Hazell et al. (2024), G. J. Hughes and Byrom 
(2019) and Spear, Morey, and van Steen (2020) scored 100%; Ramluggun et al. (2022) 
scored 95%; and Kendall (2017) and Payne (2022) scored 85%. The appraisal revealed that 
the studies included in this systematic review were of very high quality, with clear aims, 
high quality methodology, appropriate research design, rigorous analysis and clear 
findings.
In order to provide a more robust evaluation of the selected studies, a further 
critical appraisal was conducted using the Mixed Method’s Appraisal Tool (Hong et al.  
2018) – please see Table 4. The appraisal was conducted by assessing each study 
against the screening questions and then the questions assigned per research type. Of 
all the studies, five were assessed against qualitative category of study design and one

To conduct a thematic analysis, the ‘results’ or ‘findings’ sections from each study were 
extracted and inserted into a Word document. The thematic analysis followed the 
methods set out by Braun and Clarke in their six-step guide (Braun and Clarke 2008). An 
inductive approach was used for this thematic analysis. First, the two reviewers famil­
iarised themselves with the data. This was done by reading and re-reading the six studies 
to understand the contents and potential themes. The next step in the thematic analysis 
included initial line by line coding to generate the initial codes. This was generated by the 
first reviewer, and was independently checked by the second reviewer. The code was 
usually presented by a short phrase such as ‘academics can recognise anxiety in students’. 
In the third step, these codes were organised by grouping them and merging them to 
define and name themes by the first reviewer. The fourth step involved reviewing these 
themes and further refining them. To ensure rigour, these themes were reviewed further 
and edited by the second reviewer before agreement on the final themes and sub-themes

2.6. Synthesised findings
The thematic analysis identified four main themes from the selected studies. This includes 
recognition of MHPs, management of MHPs, adverse effects on academics and support 
from the university. Some of these themes were similar to the themes recognised in the 
reviews studied but are not exactly the same or may have more or fewer themes included. 
The themes identified from this thematic analysis are unique to this review. By applying 
Bronfenbrenner’s Ecological Systems Theory, each of these themes are affected by 
processes occurring at different levels.
3. Results
3.1. Included studies
Six studies met the inclusion criteria and were included in this systematic review. Figure 1 
illustrates the number of studies that were screened and excluded at each stage of the 
search process. A total of 10,832 studies were identified from the initial search. After initial

3.1.1. Recognition of mental health problems
Five studies considered whether academics can recognise MHPs in their students in some 
capacity (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al. 2022; Payne 2022; Hazell 
et al. 2024; G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019). Recognition in this context pertains to whether 
an academic can identify whether a student is struggling with their mental health, broadly 
differentiate between the common types of MHPs (e.g. depression and anxiety), and 
identify risk factors and triggers.
Academics can recognise that there are MHPs present in the student population, that 
self-harm or suicidal ideation is present and that the most common types seen are 
anxiety, stress and depression (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020).
The overwhelming issues that students are presenting with are varying degrees of depression 
and anxiety. (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020)
Students with depression and anxiety is the most common. (Spear, Morey, and van Steen

Social anxiety has been recognised as a big problem by academics:
The big issue that’s emerging is students diagnosed with social anxiety. (Spear, Morey, and 
van Steen 2020)
Academics can also recognise certain triggers to these MHPs:
For the younger ones, the change when they get to university is often the tigger. (Spear, 
Morey, and van Steen 2020)
Contradictory to this, other academics have experienced problems with identifying 
triggers to MHPs as one states:
Some staff need help to even begin talking about mental health and spotting triggers. (Payne  
2022)
Furthermore, there are some issues that have been recognised when it comes to making 
the distinction between normal emotions and MHPs:
There is a difference between stress and mental health problems that needs to be relayed to 
both staff and students. (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020)
Moreover, academics are found to not be sure ‘if the mental health issue is significant 
enough to cause concern regarding their own safety’ (Ramluggun et al. 2022). Other 
academics can’t identify certain mental health concerns:
I don’t know what a low mental health concern is. (Payne 2022)
I’m not sure what early warning signs might be. (Payne 2022)
In relation to being able to identify MHPs, Hazell et al. (2024) finds that it is down to 
students to identify their own MHP and raise it as an issue due to the academics’ inability 
to do so.
One student stated: ‘if I wasn’t explicit about my concerns and anxiety I don’t know that 
I would receive the same support [. . .] They [supervisors] generally don’t seem so aware or 
able to pre-empt them’. (Hazell et al. 2024)
Further problems in recognising MHPs have been noted in some special situations. One of 
these was the COVID-19 pandemic which made it hard for staff to identify students facing 
MHPs:
Now that things are online (in terms of academic advisor role), I find it challenging and 
worrying in identifying if students are struggling with their mental health – as everything is 
more “remote” and I miss “ad hoc”/opportunistic catch-ups with students (e.g. when face-to- 
face teaching and tutoring). (Ramluggun et al. 2022)
Another situation presenting difficulties with identifying MHPs in students is when 
students are on courses (such as healthcare courses), requiring them to go to placement 
and away from the university environment:
We have students isolated on placement in the sense that there will only be two or three of 
them working out in a hospital. (G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019)
Overall, the recognition of MHPs by academics is affected at a microsystem with their 
own ability to recognise MHPs and triggers. This can also be influenced by processes

13
occurring at the macrosystem such as societal attitudes and how that impacts the 
academic’s ability to recognise MHPs since more awareness amongst society will 
impact their own MHL. At the chronosystem level, COVID-19 has meant that remote 
learning has made it harder for academics to recognise MHPs (Ramluggun et al. 2022). 
This can be due to the fact there is less face to face interaction with students, so 
academics are less prone to developing relationships with their students and monitor­
ing their mental health.
3.1.2. Management of mental health problems
All included studies discussed how academics provide support to students with 
MHPs. The types of support that academics were providing in the studies were 
identified to include talking to their students, making reasonable adjustments for 
them and signposting students for further specialised help. However, there are 
a variation of findings presented in the studies with relation to the support that 
students receive from academics. According to Spear, Morey, and van Steen (2020), 
most academics claim to be comfortable in dealing with MHPs in students – with the 
mean response being 2.14, where 1 = strongly agree and 5 = strongly disagree. 
Moreover, the following statements reveal that academics are willing to support 
their students.
I am happy to talk with students, be with them during difficult times, and sort out practical 
problems. (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020)
I would also like to hear the voice of the students and discuss what they would like. What 
would help and support them. (Kendall 2017)
Students equally discussed the support their academics provided to them:
My supervisor is aware of my anxiety and has been supportive but not pushy . . . my super­
visor allows me to take lead on contact and meetings, with gentle reminders if I become 
distant for too long. (Hazell et al. 2024)
On the other hand, there are some limitations recognised with regards to the support that 
academics provide. For example, academics are not always confident in supporting 
students with MHPs (Ramluggun et al. 2022), and some are unsure on how to make 
reasonable adjustments for their students (Kendall 2017). Students have also voiced 
wanting the academics to seem more interested in their MHPs, have more individual 
meetings, be more caring, empathic, listen to them more and have basic knowledge of 
mental health conditions (Payne 2022). One student stated about the support they 
received:
If I wasn’t explicit about my concerns and anxiety I don’t know that I would receive the same 
support. (Hazell et al. 2024)
Academics also spoke about the difficulties they experienced in providing support to 
students. On some courses, such as healthcare courses, academics feel limited with the 
help they can offer to students due to professional regulations placed by regulating 
bodies (G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019). Moreover, in some situations where students resist 
speaking about their MHPs, academics found difficulties in the support that they could 
offer:
14
A. MOIN AND O. AKINLOTAN
Whilst some students are happy to discuss how they can be helped, others won’t . . . it makes 
it really difficult. I want to help them but what can you do? (Kendall 2017)
Other academics think that at times they cannot meet students’ expectations:
They do misunderstand and overestimate sometimes the possibilities of reasonable adjust­
ment. (Kendall 2017)
I feel that students’ expectations are not being met in this sense. They are told one thing by 
support staff and another by the link lecturer and placement area. (Ramluggun et al. 2022)
The relationship between an academic and student has been identified as an important 
factor in determining the support that academics provide to students as identified by 
Ramluggun et al. (2022), Kendall (2017), Payne (2022) and Hazell et al. (2024). Students 
deem staff to be more accessible to them than counselling (Payne 2022). This is important 
as a good relationship with a supervisor can really help with the mental wellbeing of 
a student (Hazell et al. 2024), as one student states:
The relationship with my supervisor does help me a lot when I have wellbeing difficulties. 
(Hazell et al. 2024)
Not having a good relationship can pose difficulties as sometimes stigma may arise which 
can make talking about the subject of mental health difficult as one student states:
Staff should be aware of their own stigma. Some people have had it lucky, had a decent 
upbringing, and do not really know their body language and expressions can be put onto 
students without consciously realising this. (Payne 2022)
He frequently (perhaps without meaning to) creates an atmosphere of fear and intimidation 
[. . .] it also means I refuse to tell him anything about myself or my personal life or my 
wellbeing. (Hazell et al. 2024)
Some academics also feel as if students are reluctant to share their own MHPs because of 
the associated stigma (Ramluggun et al. 2022). This can cause issues because academics 
are then restricted to the information they have about students by being dependent on 
what the student wants to tell them (Ramluggun et al. 2022). This can be difficult for 
academics who revealed that problems arise when students do not disclose their issues 
during assessment times (Kendall 2017).
Academics also find difficulties in signposting students due to limitations in their 
understanding, as one academic said:
I was unsure what to say if I called the counselling team [on the student’s behalf]. If it is 
suggested to the student to do this, my feeling is they may not; and [It] depends on whether 
[it is] a disability thing or a wellbeing thing. I would hope to put them in touch with the right 
people but would not really know. (Payne 2022)
A strong academic–student relationship has been identified to add further difficulty in the 
academic’s ability to signpost: 
The thing I find most difficult is that, we do have a good relationship with our students, 
because we teach in small classes and we cap the number of students on our course and so 
we always have very small cohorts in front of us, so you know them really well and one of the 
difficulties is, when they come to you, they don’t really want you to send them somewhere 
else. (G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019)
JOURNAL OF FURTHER AND HIGHER EDUCATION
15
At the microsystem, academics report variation in the support they provide for students 
due to their own understanding of MHPs (Payne 2022) and at the mesosystem, often the 
academic’s relationship with the student impacts this (Hazell et al. 2024). The ecosystem 
also impacts this as academics must stick to certain policies for specific courses which can 
affect the support they offer to students with MHPs. Societal attitudes and stigma which 
can be seen at the macrosystem level can also impact the student or the academic, thus 
making it harder to manage MHPs (Payne 2022). These societal attitudes and stigma can 
manifest as students feeling fearful to bring up their issues.
3.1.3. Adverse effects on the academics
The mention of adverse effects on academics because of their support to students with 
MHPs is identified in four studies (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al.  
2022; Payne 2022; G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019). In this context, ‘adverse effects’ refers to 
any unwanted or harmful outcomes faced by the academic from providing support to 
students in relation to their MHPs. The two main adverse effects include: 1) the amount of 
time academics have to spend on students which impacts on their other roles and 2) the 
emotional impacts they have to deal with.
A lack of time and consequent disruption in wider roles has been identified (Spear, 
Morey, and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al. 2022; G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019). 
Academics disagree that they have sufficient time to support students with MHPs and 
thought they should be doing other work-related activities when they were doing this 
(Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020). This can be attributed to the additional administra­
tion and communication involved (Ramluggun et al. 2022). Academics claim that due to 
the time-consuming nature of supporting students, their workload is affected; as one 
academic states, they ‘often have to work extra to catch up on other work’, and ‘workloads 
increase exponentially when we need to support students’ mental health’ (Ramluggun et al.  
2022). Academics further reported about supporting students:
I feel (as a module leader) that we do not always have the time to look into each one [student] 
individually. (Ramluggun et al. 2022)
But when they drop in and they are crying, actually me saying “I need to do this marking and 
you haven’t made an appointment” it is not helpful. So, you end up seeing them and then, 
yeah, every-thing else just has to wait. (G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019)
Another stated: ‘it is also incredibly time consuming and disruptive to work’ (Spear, Morey, 
and van Steen 2020).
The issue of academics facing an emotional toll from supporting students appears in 
three studies (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al. 2022; Payne 2022). 
Academics can experience MHPs (particularly stress and worry) from dealing with the 
responsibility of serious MHPs present in students as evident in the following quotes from 
academics:
It is terrifying feeling responsible for a suicidal student and worrying if you have given the 
right advice. (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020)
If you’re dealing with students who are experiencing paranoia or depression, then managing 
that student in the classroom . . . is going to have an impact on your own mental health and 
sense of wellbeing and confidence. (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020)
16

Academics also mentioned how it affects them mentally beyond their contact time with 
the student:
Sometimes it is overwhelming, stories from some of the students that can be disturbing [. . .] 
I found some of the stories quite upsetting. Thought about it for a good while afterwards. 
(Ramluggun et al. 2022)
Yes it does exert an emotional strain by being concerned about their wellbeing. (Ramluggun 
et al. 2022)
Students also recognise the effects this was having on academics:
I think the lecturers need an outlet either as a group or privately. (Payne 2022)
Another student worried that staff ‘seemed to be impacted by providing the support’ (Payne  
2022).
At the microsystem level, academics may have certain personal experiences and 
opinions that can emotionally impact them with the support they provide for 
students (Ramluggun et al. 2022). The ecosystem can strongly influence the adverse 
effects faced by academics including the time-consuming nature of providing sup­
port and the disruption to their wider roles (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020). 
Factors such as standard workloads, contact hours, and policies can impact this. At 
the macrosystem, cultural attitudes such as work culture can negatively impact this 
as well.
3.1.4. Support from the university
The issue about support from universities has been identified in five studies (Spear, Morey, 
and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al. 2022; Kendall 2017; Payne 2022; G. J. Hughes and 
Byrom 2019). It relates to how the university supports the academics with them support­
ing students with MHPs. It is an important factor contributing to MHL in academics as 
identified in the studies. The key sub-themes pertaining to the university’s support are: 
training from the university, university guidance and clearer definition of roles.
3.1.4.1. Training. The need to train academics to support students with MHPS is iden­
tified in four studies (Kendall 2017; Payne 2022; Ramluggun et al. 2022; Spear, Morey, and 
van Steen 2020). According to research by Spear, Morey, and van Steen (2020), 31% of 
academics do not think their institution has adequately prepared them to work with 
students with MHPs. Moreover, of the academics who did not receive training, 56% would 
attend if it was offered (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020). Similarly, according to 
Ramluggun et al. (2022), academics do not feel prepared or confident in supporting 
students with MHPs. Most academics claim to not have received any training in relation 
to supporting students with MHPs and that they would benefit from training opportu­
nities (Ramluggun et al. 2022). Kendall (2017) also finds that every academic in their study 
acknowledged wanting more training in the area of mental health. Another major 
recommendation put forward by academics as identified by Payne (2022) is that staff 
want more accessible and flexible training to support students. Similar sentiments are 
echoed in the following statements:
JOURNAL OF FURTHER AND HIGHER EDUCATION
17
Mental health first aid training or something similar would be very helpful. (Ramluggun et al.  
2022)
I don’t think that I have the time to embark on something in depth and lengthy, but 
something short and smart. It’s important to know the basics in order to support your 
students. (Kendall 2017)
Students also recommend that staff should ‘understand some examples of mental health 
conditions, like different types of stress or anxiety, or know the differences between stress and 
anxiety, to know the symptoms’. (Payne 2022).
Due to the lack of training discussed above, students stated that some academics were 
ill-prepared in supporting them (Payne 2022). However, staff are still happy to offer help 
even if they don’t feel trained as evident in the following statement by an academic:
I’m not trained, I’m not a professional, I’m not qualified, but I’ll do what I can. (Spear, Morey, 
and van Steen 2020)
Additionally, academics have been identified to often rely on their knowledge from their 
professional practices rather than any professional training:
I often fall back on using previous experiences of supporting depressed patients. (Ramluggun 
et al. 2022)
3.1.4.2. University guidance. Four studies (Kendall 2017; Payne 2022; Ramluggun et al.  
2022; Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020) discussed the guidance provided by universities 
to academics in supporting students with MHPs. Overall, there is a sense of a lack of 
support from universities when it comes to the guidance and procedures available to 
academics. Spear, Morey, and van Steen (2020) finds that academics receive little gui­
dance and as a result, ‘students and staff do not know where to find help which is why some 
academics become very involved with supporting individuals’ (Spear, Morey, and van Steen  
2020). Ramluggun et al. (2022) finds that academics would like more clarity on formal 
processes and procedures within the university in supporting students. They further 
suggest that implementing university procedures to support students are at times 
impractical and dependent on their job role, for example certain jobs such as being 
module leader means that the university procedures are too time consuming to put into 
practice (Ramluggun et al. 2022). Others feel that current university procedures in sup­
porting students are prescriptive and inflexible, causing frustration by restricting aca­
demics in the support they provide or by delaying it. One academic claimed that:
It is sometimes frustrating when I would like to do more but have to abide by university rules 
(i.e. re exceptional circumstances when student needing extra time). (Ramluggun et al. 2022)
Academics commented on having little access to student information in order to support 
them, and would like information (such as individual support plans) to be shared amongst 
colleagues for consistency in the support the student receives (Ramluggun et al. 2022). They 
also state that support plans in place for students with MHPs are unrealistic and challenging 
to manage in practice (Ramluggun et al. 2022). Similarly, Kendall (2017) finds that academics 
do not feel supported by the learning support plans that are in place for students, although 
some do consider it to be a good resource, it lacks detail, has inaccurate recommendations 
and does not reflect actual requirements. The academics said about this:

They [LSPs] are very generic, especially in relation to mental health issues . . . there is nothing 
that helps me to support the student. They are absolutely useless; I would prefer the plans to 
be more specific. (Kendall 2017)
There is also a lack of reinforcement by university systems and policies in supporting 
students with MHPs as experienced by staff according to Payne (2022). Moreover, aca­
demics report wanting clearer guidelines on what is expected of them as one academic 
said in the following statement:
What is the university expecting us to do on the front line? Guidelines on how to record these 
encounters would be helpful. Confidentiality is about contact details for students, but not 
about conversations with us. Be more specific, give clearer guidance. (Payne 2022)
It is further identified that staff often report feeling not having someone to consult or 
speak to for support within the university. Academics mentioned feeling alone in support­
ing students (Ramluggun et al. 2022) and wanting someone to speak to for advice, clarity, 
peace of mind and to prevent isolation as evident in the following statements by 
academics:
I sometimes feel I need advice, someone to talk to make sure I am doing the right thing, and 
a “specialist”/colleague in this area would be useful to identify for supervision. (Ramluggun et al.  
2022)
I think having an opportunity to talk through decisions with a colleague is helpful. 
(Ramluggun et al. 2022)
To speak to someone knowledgeable for support; to have someone with whom things could 
be thought through, clarifying the right action; and the need for some support for staff, 
I would say staff resilience comes into this, and wellbeing. (Payne 2022)
One academic even said with regards to having someone to go to for advice that: ‘I thought it 
is more useful than going on a one day training course’. (Kendall 2017)
3.1.4.3. Clearer definition of roles. The issue of clear definitions of roles for academics 
has been identified in four studies (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al.  
2022; Payne 2022; G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019). According to Ramluggun et al. (2022), 
academics stated that there are ‘blurred boundaries’ due to the multiple roles that they 
hold. In this study, one academic recognised this by saying ‘since I, myself, am a mental 
health therapist I have to balance the fact that this is not my role with the students’ 
(Ramluggun et al. 2022). Similarly, in another study by Payne (2022), academics with 
additional clinical roles questioned:
Where does our professional duty stop? If I really do feel somebody is at risk, where I would 
still have responsibility as a nurse, so I would need to do something about that, but I wouldn’t 
want to overstep my academic, erm, role as well. (Payne 2022)
There has also been a mention of difficulty in keeping the two roles separate:
It is difficult because we are not just academic staff, we are on a professional register as well 
and it is difficult to keep these two things apart and not, sort of, try to do too much in the role 
as personal tutor. (G. J. Hughes and Byrom 2019)
JOURNAL OF FURTHER AND HIGHER EDUCATION
19
Academics identified that it is important to set boundaries, but also feel that at the same 
time, they are pushing their students away (Ramluggun et al. 2022). One academic added: 
‘I think that there needs to be very clear lines and boundaries of the types of support we 
should offer and when the role stops falling to us’ (Ramluggun et al. 2022).
Spear, Morey, and van Steen (2020) also demonstrated that academics had little 
guidance about what their role should be in supporting students. An academic in this 
study stated that: ‘I am not a medical doctor or a social worker or a carer, it is not my job to 
support people with mental health issues’ (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020).
Overall, support from the university is influenced by processes occurring at many 
different levels. The mesosystem level can demonstrate how beneficial it is for academics 
to interact with each other as a source of support (Ramluggun et al. 2022). At the 
ecosystem level, the economic state of the university can affect mental health priorities, 
and resources available to support academics dealing with MHPS. Furthermore, at this 
level, there is a lack of training, guidance and funding for universities. This can be 
explained by processes at the macrosystem, including cultural attitudes which don’t see 
this as a priority to be addressed.
4. Discussion
This review identified that there is a general lack of support from universities sensed by 
academics. A lack of training for academics was highlighted and this can be explained by 
the ecological systems theory. Processes such as training and guidelines at the ecosystem 
level are affected by those at the macro-level including societal attitudes and national 
campaigns. The studies in this review all discussed the need for training and clear 
guidance. Many academics reported not feeling prepared when it came to managing 
students with MHPs or not having received training to be able to do this (Spear, Morey, 
and van Steen 2020; Ramluggun et al. 2022; Kendall 2017; Payne 2022). Additionally, 
academics report feeling unclear about the limit of their involvement when supporting 
such students due to a lack of clarity regarding their role (Akinlotan and Wagstaff 2025). 
Academics also expressed being open to receiving training in this area. This is in con­
cordance with common discourses about this issue, such as in a report published by the 
charity Students Minds (G. Hughes et al. 2018), who also report insufficient training for 
academics and an openness amongst them to receive this. The ecological systems theory 
demonstrates how a lack of training at the ecosystem level affects the mesosystem by 
affecting the relationship the academic holds with the student and at the microsystem 
level with the academic’s own personal attitudes being influenced.
Furthermore, unclear guidance from universities is another problem faced by aca­
demics. This concern was raised during the University of Bristol v. Abrahart case 
(Equality and Human Rights Commission 2024), in which a university student committed 
suicide due to her MHPs. Subsequently, the court found that the university had ‘failed to 
make reasonable adjustments’ for the student and that ‘she had been indirectly discrimi­
nated against and discriminated against on the grounds of disability’ (Equality and Human 
Rights Commission 2024). This is highlighted as a problem as universities have an 
expectation from academics to support their students (Kantaris 2018). Students have 
also recommended that universities should do more to raise awareness of services and 
procedures in place to seek help (Priestley et al. 2021), which is in agreement with the

findings in this systematic review where academics often did not know where to go for 
help. Academics in the UK report wanting better understanding of their role and the need 
to establish boundaries (Kantaris 2018). The ecological systems theory explains how the 
absence of sufficient support from universities for academics puts them in a difficult 
situation when managing students with MHPs (Thompson et al. 2021). It can put aca­
demics in dangerous positions in which they may struggle to navigate appropriately 
managing MHPs (Douglas et al. 2023). Brewster et al. (2021) similarly claims that support­
ing academics is vital for them to support students.
Inadequate training and support from universities for academics can explain the 
adverse effects staff members experience in supporting students at the microsystem 
level (Payne 2022; Ramluggun et al. 2022; Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020). This review 
has found that supporting students is very time consuming and disruptive to academics’ 
wider roles and affects their own mental wellbeing. Similarly, this was found in other 
studies such as that by Margrove, Gustowska, and Grove (2011). Staff have also suggested 
they are usually de-prioritised when it comes to wellbeing and that this is likely to persist 
(Douglas et al. 2023). It is important to address this before the issue worsens. This can be 
achieved through investing in more mental health support as put forward by students in 
one study (Priestley et al. 2021). Studies have found that lack of training exacerbates 
academics’ workloads (Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020). The same reasoning can be 
applied for the emotional toll academics face from supporting students. With training and 
support from the university, there might be less anxiety among staff because they would 
be able to better deal with their emotions, not feel so isolated in supporting students, 
minimise double questioning themselves, and not be so intimidated by the responsibility 
of dealing with more serious MHPs as they currently are (Payne 2022, Ramluggun et al.  
2022; Spear, Morey, and van Steen 2020). This study found that a whole university 
approach is necessary in improving MHL amongst academics. Likewise, Brewster et al. 
(2021) advocate for a whole university approach in promoting staff and student 
wellbeing.
Other consequences include that students are not receiving the support that they need 
(Batchelor et al. 2019). This issue is of greater importance in the current climate where 
universities are seeing a rise in the number of students presenting with MHPs (Sanders  
2023). There is an increasing demand to meet the needs of these students (Macaskill  
2012). Training and guidance would be an appropriate measure to bring in place – this 
has also been recognised in other research studies such as that by Sampson et al. (2022).
By applying the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (Damschroder 
et al., 2022), various contextual factors affecting the MHL of academics can be highlighted. 
Firstly, in terms of the intervention characteristics, the results show that interventions 
offered by academics differ between them and are highly dependent on their own 
personal expertise and experience. Some interventions offered include speaking to 
students, signposting them to further services and enabling reasonable adjustments for 
them. By exploring the outer setting, some of the external influences acting on the MHL of 
academics are evident. The ecological systems theory shows that this includes the sup­
port that the government offers such as the ‘University Mental Health Charter 
Programme’ and guidance regarding suicide at university (The Education Hub 2024) 
helping to support and promote mental health at universities. Additionally, the Office 
for Students has funded Student Space which acts as a mental health and wellbeing hub 
JOURNAL OF FURTHER AND HIGHER EDUCATION
21
(Office for Students 2025). COVID-19 was also an important external influence made 
evident at the chronosystem level, as the subsequent social distancing meant that 
universities had to resort to remote learning which studies found to be a source of 
worsening mental health and isolation (Lister, Seale, and Douce 2021). It also means 
that academics have limited access to students, making it harder to identify any MHPs 
(Tyler 2025). Furthermore, stigma is an important influencing factor affecting MHL in 
academics as it is reported that 51% of UK adults believe there is some sort of shame 
associated with MHPs (Mind 2024). This can hinder the support that academics can 
provide to students as they may feel hesitant in doing so or the student may feel ashamed 
in accepting this.
By exploring the inner setting, which includes the university environment, many 
contextual factors affecting MHL are evident. Firstly, at the macrosystem of the ecological 
systems theory, universities are facing significant financial cuts with the sector’s net 
income expected to be £3.4 billion lower in 2025–26 as compared to previous deficits 
forecasts of £1.6 billion (Parliamentary Office of Science and Technology 2024). 
Additionally, many academics are facing redundancy with a reported 10,300 jobs being 
lost across the sector in the academic year 2023–24 (Times Higher Education 2024). The 
results of this review echo these statistics, as universities are unable to fund training 
academics, providing guidance, and enforcing mechanisms to support MHPs. 
Additionally, at the ecosystem level, academics are experiencing an increased workload, 
possibly due to taking on extra responsibilities as a result of redundancies. This review 
found academics can be emotionally impacted by the support that they provide for 
students. This can be further exacerbated or caused by the fact that academics are 
more likely to experience depression and anxiety (Devendorf, Wilson, and Ingram 2024), 
with one report finding that 53% of academics surveyed showed signs of depression 
(University and College Union 2024). Finally, the results from this systematic review 
revealed that academics are best able to support students when they have training, 
guidance/procedures to follow, support from other academics as well as having protected 
time for supporting students and their own academic work.
In order to address these barriers and facilitate change, Kotter’s Change Management 
Model (Kotter 2007) can be applied to analyse the mental health support offered by 
academics at universities. Firstly, a sense of urgency already exists given that MHPs 
amongst university students is established as an important issue that needs to be 
addressed. Next, the existence of a guiding coalition could be stronger, although national 
and university policies exist to address MHPs amongst students, the collaboration 
between them and academics is minimal. Next, a stronger vision and strategy is needed; 
although there are national and university policies and guidelines in place, these do not 
offer clear plans or strategies that are known by academics or students. Simpson and 
Ferguson (2014) suggest that a student should be made aware multiple times and 
through different ways throughout their university journey in order to raise awareness 
about mental health support that is available. Gorczynski et al. (2017) similarly suggest 
making online anonymous resources available to students. The next step explores 
empowering others to act on the vision; unfortunately, there are many obstacles in the 
way of academics achieving this. These obstacles are present at a national, university and 
individual level. They include funding troubles (Times Higher Education 2024), workload 
pressures on the academic and personal emotional difficulties (Spear, Morey, and van

Steen 2020). Next, planning and creating short-term wins could look like individual 
universities tracking the mental health of their students through surveys before and 
after providing the appropriate training and guidance to academics. To consolidate and 
sustain improved MHL amongst academics, it is important to remove the obstacles by 
reconsidering mental health priorities at national and institutional levels.
4.1. Strengths
This systematic review has some strengths. Firstly, it followed the PRISMA protocol which 
ensures transparency and reproducibility. Secondly, the search strategy was very exten­
sive in which many different databases were used, and an exhaustive list of search terms 
were utilised to ensure that as many studies could be considered as possible. The studies 
were also evaluated using the CASP checklist to ensure the studies’ methodologies were 
trustworthy and relevant. This is also the first systematic review, at the time of writing this, 
which synthesises all primary data available exploring the MHL of academics at British 
universities. Furthermore, this systematic review has been conducted in a timely manner, 
where in the past year, the mental health of university students has been of growing 
concern and making headlines due to findings of a rise in MHPs (Sanders 2023).
4.2. Limitations
One limitation of this systematic review is that the search was restricted to studies 
published in the past 10  years. Restricting the time-period might mean there are 
studies published before 2014 that are excluded. This can be a limitation considering 
that some of these sources could have possibly provided valuable additional insight 
into the MHL of academics at British universities. On the other hand, these studies 
would not be recent. Therefore, implementing these restrictions allow for a more 
focused discussion and recommendation according to the UK’s current context. 
Secondly, this systematic review had a limited number of studies that it included, 
with only six being eligible for use. After an exhaustive search, only six studies fit the 
eligibility criteria. This reflects a research gap in this subject area and therefore draws 
a need for more primary research to be conducted into the MHL of academics. 
However, the studies that were included were of high quality according to evaluation 
by the CASP checklist and Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool, so this systematic review has 
synthesised information that is trustworthy and relevant.
4.3. Conclusion
This systematic review recognises that there is insufficient training and support from 
universities despite the increasing role academics have in offering mental health support 
to students. Training, guidance and support for academics is the most important aspect to 
be addressed. It can be linked to the problem of inconsistent support provided to 
students by academics, confusion about roles and responsibilities, the disruption in 
wider activities and emotional problems experienced by staff. Ensuring that staff are 
adequately equipped with the knowledge, skills and structures by the university can 
JOURNAL OF FURTHER AND HIGHER EDUCATION
23
significantly improve MHL and reduce adverse effects. It is therefore vital to support 
academics at an institutional and national level so that they can support students.
4.4. Recommendations
Based on the results from this review, there are a few areas to focus on to improve MHL 
amongst British academics. These overarching recommendations include national poli­
cies looking after academics, increased focus and support from universities towards 
academics supporting students with MHPS and more research into this area.
Firstly, there are structural factors that need to be addressed for academics to feel 
empowered enough to support students. This systematic review found that academics 
are overworked with limited time to support students, this can be attributed to factors 
occurring at a macrosystem level. This includes the significant financial cuts in the 
university sector (Parliamentary Office of Science and Technology 2024) and redundancies 
(Times Higher Education 2024). Academics also have been reported to be more likely to 
experience depression (University and College Union 2024). Improving working condi­
tions for academics by reducing staff redundancy, the subsequent immense workload, 
and mental health pressures they face, can allow academics to better support their 
students.
Universities should support academics who are dealing with students with MHPs by 
offering training, guidelines/policies and support from other colleagues, as this review has 
found that academics are not adequately trained in MHPs. Universities can implement this 
as mandatory training for student-facing staff. The training should inform academics how 
to recognise MHPs, differentiate it from normal emotions, communicate with students, 
develop trust with students, direct them on how to signpost and how to look after 
themselves. Furthermore, a recommendation put forward after the University of Bristol 
v. Abrahart case is relevant here and should be considered, which is that the behaviour of 
students should be considered in order to recognise potential MHPs. This includes their 
attendance, engagement with courses and activities, submission of work and discrepan­
cies between certain modules or formats (Equality and Human Rights Commission 2024). 
Monitoring students’ attendance and engagement can allow universities to identify 
worrisome changes. Simpson and Ferguson (2014) also suggest that students should be 
made aware multiple times about the support available for them. This can take the form 
of online anonymous resources as recommended by Gorczynski et al. (2017).
Furthermore, there is a need for clear guidelines that detail the boundaries of 
the roles of academics as mental health support systems, how involved they 
should be and about structures in place at universities that support academics. 
As recommended by the University of Bristol v. Abrahart case (Equality and Human 
Rights Commission 2024), a list of common reasonable adjustments should be 
provided for academics and other student-facing staff. Brewster et al. (2021) 
advocate for a whole university approach in promoting staff and student well­
being. In line with this, universities can benefit from appointing an academic in 
each department who is adequately trained in managing MHPs in students; they 
should be accessible to other academics in the department who may have ques­
tions or require further guidance or just want to talk to a colleague about 
a student they are supporting. If there are no such structures at universities

supporting academics, this is an area of concern and should be a priority to 
address. Academics should feel well supported by their universities and feel that 
they can raise concerns or seek help both in supporting students and for their own 
wellbeing.
Finally, more research needs to be conducted in this area. Ideally the research should 
be primary and should aim to increase the number of universities, subject areas and types 
of higher education (i.e. undergraduate, postgraduate) that it considers. It should also aim 
to include the thoughts and perspective of both students and academics.
